content.text: Standard self-attention computes a score for every pair of tokens. For a sequence of length

matrix — O(n²) in both time and memory. A sequence of 100 tokens produces a 10,000-element attention matrix. A sequence of 100,000 tokens (long document) would need 10 billion elements. Efficient transformers address this quadratic bottleneck through several complementary strategies.

attrs.alt: Efficient transformer techniques: sparse/local attention, linear attention, FlashAttention tiling, grouped-query attention, and summary comparison table

content.text: The simplest reduction: instead of attending to all
positions, each token attends only to a
. This reduces complexity to O(n·w). Longformer (Beltagy et al. 2020) combines local windowed attention with a handful of global tokens (e.g.
for classification) that can attend to everything:

from transformers import LongformerModel, LongformerTokenizer
import torch

tokenizer = LongformerTokenizer.from_pretrained('allenai/longformer-base-4096')
model     = LongformerModel.from_pretrained('allenai/longformer-base-4096')

text = "Long document text here " * 200  # >512 tokens
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors='pt', max_length=4096, truncation=True)

# attention_mask: 1=local, 2=global
# Make [CLS] (token 0) attend globally
attention_mask = inputs['attention_mask'].clone()
attention_mask[0, 0] = 2  # global attention for [CLS]

with torch.no_grad():
    out = model(**inputs, attention_mask=attention_mask)
print(out.last_hidden_state.shape)  # (1, T, 768)
with a window of 4,096 tokens. Information from beyond the window can still propagate across layers — after L layers, a token can effectively attend up to L·w positions away.
FlashAttention (Dao et al. 2022) does not reduce the theoretical O(n²) operation count — it still computes the exact same attention matrix. Instead it eliminates the bottleneck of
slow GPU memory (HBM) reads and writes

. Standard attention materialises the full n × n matrix in HBM; FlashAttention tiles the computation into blocks that fit in fast on-chip SRAM (shared memory), computing the result block-by-block without ever writing the full matrix to HBM.
: the softmax normaliser is computed incrementally as blocks are processed, using the "safe softmax" identity that lets you update a running max and running sum without re-scanning prior blocks.

attrs.latex: \text{softmax}(x_1, \ldots, x_n)_i = \frac{e^{x_i - m}}{\sum_j e^{x_j - m}}, \quad m = \max_j x_j

content.text: In practice, FlashAttention is 2–4× faster than standard PyTorch attention at sequence length 2,048 and the speedup grows with sequence length. It is now the default in most LLM training frameworks and is enabled in PyTorch 2.0 via

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

B, H, T, D = 2, 12, 2048, 64
Q = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16)
K = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16)
V = torch.randn(B, H, T, D, device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16)

# PyTorch 2.0 automatically uses FlashAttention if available
# is_causal=True applies the causal mask efficiently (no explicit mask tensor needed)
with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(enable_flash=True):
    out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, is_causal=True)

print(out.shape)  # (2, 12, 2048, 64)

# Verify FlashAttention is being used
with torch.backends.cuda.sdp_kernel(enable_flash=True, enable_math=False, enable_mem_efficient=False):
    # This will raise if FlashAttention is not available
    out2 = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, is_causal=True)
At inference time, the main bottleneck is often not computation but the

— the stored keys and values for all previous tokens in the context. In standard multi-head attention (MHA) with H heads, the KV cache grows as 2 · H · T · d_head bytes per layer. For LLaMA-65B with H=64 heads and a 4K context, this is tens of gigabytes.
: all H query heads share a single K and V head. KV cache shrinks by H×. Used in Falcon and PaLM.
: Q heads are split into G groups; each group shares one K and V head. GQA with G=1 is MQA; G=H is MHA. LLaMA-2/3 and Mistral use G=8 (8 KV heads for 32 Q heads).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class GroupedQueryAttention(nn.Module):
    """GQA: num_heads query heads, num_kv_heads key/value heads."""
    def __init__(self, d_model, num_heads, num_kv_heads):
        super().__init__()
        assert num_heads % num_kv_heads == 0
        self.num_heads    = num_heads
        self.num_kv_heads = num_kv_heads
        self.groups       = num_heads // num_kv_heads
        self.d_head       = d_model // num_heads

        self.q_proj  = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.k_proj  = nn.Linear(d_model, num_kv_heads * self.d_head, bias=False)
        self.v_proj  = nn.Linear(d_model, num_kv_heads * self.d_head, bias=False)
        self.o_proj  = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        Q = self.q_proj(x).view(B, T, self.num_heads,    self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)
        K = self.k_proj(x).view(B, T, self.num_kv_heads, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)
        V = self.v_proj(x).view(B, T, self.num_kv_heads, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)

        # Expand K and V so each query head has a matching KV
        K = K.repeat_interleave(self.groups, dim=1)  # (B, num_heads, T, d_head)
        V = V.repeat_interleave(self.groups, dim=1)

        out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, is_causal=True)
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, -1)
        return self.o_proj(out)

# LLaMA-2 style: 32 query heads, 8 KV heads
gqa = GroupedQueryAttention(d_model=4096, num_heads=32, num_kv_heads=8)
print(gqa.k_proj.weight.shape)   # (8*128, 4096) — 4× smaller than MHA
Linear attention approximates softmax attention using a
. The softmax can be written as:

first (a d × d matrix independent of n), the total cost drops to O(n·d²). The Performer model uses random Fourier features to approximate the softmax kernel. In practice, linear attention models often fall slightly short of full attention quality, but recurrent formulations (Mamba, RWKV) that can be computed as O(n) scans are an active research direction.
GQA (reduces KV cache) + FlashAttention-2 or FlashAttention-3

FlashAttention gives the biggest free speedup — enable it first. GQA / MQA give the biggest memory reduction for inference — adopt them when KV cache is the bottleneck. Sparse and linear attention change the model's inductive bias and require training from scratch; use them only when the sequence lengths truly cannot fit in standard attention at all.

Efficient transformers are a family of architectural modifications to the standard transformer that reduce the time, memory, or hardware-communication cost of self-attention. The canonical transformer (Vaswani et al. 2017) uses scaled dot-product multi-head attention, which requires O(n squared) time and memory in sequence length n because every token must compute a score against every other token. For short sequences below 512 tokens, this cost is negligible. For long sequences of 8,192 to 1,000,000 or more tokens, it becomes the primary bottleneck.

The problem is multidimensional. First, the n times n attention matrix itself consumes memory: for n=16,384 tokens, 16 heads, and bfloat16 precision, the attention matrix is approximately 8.5 GB per layer, exceeding the memory of a single GPU. Second, even if the matrix fits, reading and writing it from GPU high-bandwidth memory is slow because memory bandwidth, not compute throughput, is the bottleneck for attention at typical sequence lengths. Third, distributing long-sequence computation across multiple GPUs requires all-to-all communication that scales with sequence length.

Efficient transformer variants attack these three bottlenecks through four complementary strategies: reducing the number of token pairs that compute attention (sparsity); reordering matrix operations to avoid materialising the full n times n matrix (IO-aware computation or FlashAttention); reducing the number of key-value heads while keeping query heads (grouped-query attention); and approximating the softmax to achieve linear complexity (linear or kernel attention). Modern production LLMs combine multiple strategies: LLaMA-3 uses FlashAttention plus GQA; Mistral 7B uses sliding window attention plus GQA plus FlashAttention.

Context length is directly tied to the tasks a model can perform. An 8K-token model cannot read a 50-page legal contract in one call. A 128K-token model can, but only if the attention computation fits in memory and completes within a reasonable time. Efficient transformers are what make long-context LLMs economically feasible.

At inference time, the KV cache stores the key and value vectors for all previous tokens so they do not need to be recomputed at each generation step. For LLaMA-65B with 32 KV heads, 80 layers, and a 4K context in float16, the KV cache is approximately 5.4 GB per request. GQA with 8 KV heads instead of 32 reduces this to 1.35 GB per request, quadrupling the batch size you can serve on a fixed GPU budget.

Training cost matters too. A single attention forward pass for n=32,768 tokens with standard attention requires materialising a 32,768 times 32,768 times H attention matrix and reading it from HBM twice. FlashAttention avoids this and achieves 2 to 4 times wallclock speedup with identical numerical results.
GQA plus FlashAttention enables 128K to 1M token context windows that are impractical with standard MHA.
FlashAttention eliminates the n times n activation tensor from HBM. GQA reduces the KV cache size by H divided by G. Together these can reduce per-request memory by 4 to 10 times.
FlashAttention achieves 2 to 4 times speedup on training throughput at common sequence lengths by reducing HBM bandwidth pressure.
Lower memory per request allows more requests to share a GPU, increasing serving throughput and reducing cost per token.
FlashAttention and GQA produce mathematically identical results to standard MHA within floating-point rounding. This is not an approximation.
Linear attention and state-space models achieve constant-time per-token generation regardless of context length.
Linear attention and sparse attention that excludes relevant token pairs produce lower-quality outputs than exact full attention.
FlashAttention requires custom CUDA kernels; GQA requires restructuring projection layers; sparse attention requires specialised masking logic.
FlashAttention performs best on Ampere and Hopper GPUs with specific SRAM capacities. On older hardware the speedup may be minimal.
Switching from MHA to GQA after the fact requires retraining from scratch because the projection dimensions change.
Kernel-based linear attention drops the softmax non-linearity. On tasks requiring selective attention, this approximation degrades quality noticeably.

FlashAttention should always be used. There is no reason not to enable it for any training or inference workload on supported hardware. It is exact, free, and activated automatically by F.scaled_dot_product_attention in PyTorch 2.0 and later.

GQA is appropriate for new model training where inference throughput and memory are a concern. G=8 KV heads for H=32 Q heads is the LLaMA-3 and Mistral standard and provides a good quality-efficiency trade-off.

Sliding window attention is useful when processing long documents where most relevant information is local. Technical documents, legal contracts, and sequential narrative text fit this pattern. Global tokens handle cross-document signals. Linear attention and SSMs are appropriate when sequence length exceeds 64K tokens and exact attention is infeasible.

A search company runs a document understanding service that classifies and summarises 100K-token legal briefs. Standard LLaMA-2 with MHA requires 22 GB KV cache per request at 32K context on a single A100, limiting batch size to 3. Switching to GQA via a re-projection procedure reduces KV cache to 5.5 GB per request, enabling batch size 14. This 4.7 times throughput improvement at identical model quality reduces the per-document cost by 78 percent.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


class EfficientTransformerLayer(nn.Module):
    """
    Transformer layer combining GQA and FlashAttention via PyTorch 2.0.
    Mirrors LLaMA-2 architecture: GQA with 8 KV heads for 32 Q heads.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        d_model: int = 4096,
        num_q_heads: int = 32,
        num_kv_heads: int = 8,
        d_ff: int = 11008,
    ):
        super().__init__()
        assert num_q_heads % num_kv_heads == 0
        self.num_q_heads = num_q_heads
        self.num_kv_heads = num_kv_heads
        self.groups = num_q_heads // num_kv_heads
        self.d_head = d_model // num_q_heads

        self.q_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, num_q_heads  * self.d_head, bias=False)
        self.k_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, num_kv_heads * self.d_head, bias=False)
        self.v_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, num_kv_heads * self.d_head, bias=False)
        self.o_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)

        self.gate = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff, bias=False)
        self.up   = nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff, bias=False)
        self.down = nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model, bias=False)
        self.attn_norm = nn.RMSNorm(d_model)
        self.ff_norm   = nn.RMSNorm(d_model)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, is_causal: bool = True) -> torch.Tensor:
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        residual = x
        x = self.attn_norm(x)

        Q = self.q_proj(x).view(B, T, self.num_q_heads,  self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)
        K = self.k_proj(x).view(B, T, self.num_kv_heads, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)
        V = self.v_proj(x).view(B, T, self.num_kv_heads, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)

        # GQA: repeat K, V to match Q head count
        K = K.repeat_interleave(self.groups, dim=1)
        V = V.repeat_interleave(self.groups, dim=1)

        # FlashAttention via PyTorch 2.0
        attn_out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V, is_causal=is_causal)
        attn_out = attn_out.transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, -1)
        x = residual + self.o_proj(attn_out)

        # SwiGLU feed-forward
        residual = x
        x = self.ff_norm(x)
        x = residual + self.down(F.silu(self.gate(x)) * self.up(x))
        return x


# Memory comparison: MHA vs GQA
d_model, H = 4096, 32
mha_kv_per_token = 2 * H         * (d_model // H) * 2  # bytes (bfloat16)
gqa_kv_per_token = 2 * (H // 4)  * (d_model // H) * 2  # 8 KV heads

print(f"MHA KV cache per token per layer: {mha_kv_per_token:,} bytes")
print(f"GQA KV cache per token per layer: {gqa_kv_per_token:,} bytes")
print(f"GQA saves: {1 - gqa_kv_per_token/mha_kv_per_token:.0%} of KV cache memory")

n_layers, T = 80, 32768
mha_total = mha_kv_per_token * n_layers * T / (1024**3)
gqa_total = gqa_kv_per_token * n_layers * T / (1024**3)
print(f"MHA total KV cache (80 layers, 32K tokens): {mha_total:.1f} GB")
print(f"GQA total KV cache (80 layers, 32K tokens): {gqa_total:.1f} GB")
Not enabling FlashAttention or using wrong dtype
2 to 4 times slower attention; OOM on long sequences
Use F.scaled_dot_product_attention with float16 or bfloat16 in PyTorch 2.0+
Using float32 instead of bfloat16 in attention
FlashAttention does not activate for float32; 2 times memory overhead
Cast to bfloat16; use autocast in training
Applying sliding window attention without global tokens
No cross-window information flow; fails on tasks requiring global context
Add at least one global attention token that attends to the full sequence
Model attends to future tokens; data leakage; incorrect generation
Always pass is_causal=True in autoregressive models
Assuming efficient attention changes model quality
FlashAttention and GQA are exact or near-exact; linear attention is approximate
Test quality on task-specific benchmarks when switching to sparse or linear attention
Use F.scaled_dot_product_attention in all new transformer implementations on supported hardware.
FlashAttention-2 is optimised for bfloat16. Use torch.amp.autocast in your training loop.
torch.compile with mode='max-autotune' can further improve FlashAttention throughput by 10 to 20 percent through kernel fusion.
Use NVIDIA Nsight Systems or torch.profiler to measure whether attention is actually the bottleneck before switching to a complex efficient variant.
Choose GQA group count based on serving batch size.
G=8 for H=32 is a common production default. Lower G reduces KV cache further at a small quality cost.
Validate custom attention implementations against reference implementations.
Bugs in CUDA kernels can produce silently wrong numerical results.
The reference CUDA implementation. FlashAttention-3 (2024) supports H100 Tensor Core async operations. Install via pip install flash-attn --no-build-isolation.
Provides memory-efficient attention, block-sparse attention, and sequence-parallel attention as drop-in modules. Used in Meta's LLaMA training pipeline.
Models with attn_implementation='flash_attention_2' or attn_implementation='sdpa' in from_pretrained automatically use efficient attention for LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon, and others.
An inference-serving framework that uses PagedAttention to virtualise the KV cache across requests, improving GPU utilisation by 24 times over naive serving.
For true O(n) inference, Mamba replaces attention with a selective state-space mechanism. Available via pip install mamba-ssm.
O(n squared) ops, O(n) HBM I/O
Long context inference, large batch serving
When n exceeds 64K and exact attention is infeasible

Efficient transformer implementations, particularly custom CUDA kernels like FlashAttention, introduce a new class of reliability risk: kernel correctness. Bugs in CUDA kernels can produce silently wrong numerical results that are difficult to detect without reference implementations. Always validate efficient attention against standard attention on small test cases before deploying.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math


def standard_attention(Q, K, V):
    """Reference implementation of scaled dot-product attention."""
    scale = 1.0 / math.sqrt(Q.size(-1))
    scores = torch.bmm(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) * scale
    weights = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    return torch.bmm(weights, V)


def efficient_attention(Q, K, V):
    """FlashAttention via PyTorch 2.0 kernel."""
    return F.scaled_dot_product_attention(Q, K, V)


torch.manual_seed(42)
B, T, D = 2, 64, 64
Q = torch.randn(B, T, D, dtype=torch.float32)
K = torch.randn(B, T, D, dtype=torch.float32)
V = torch.randn(B, T, D, dtype=torch.float32)

ref = standard_attention(Q, K, V)
eff = efficient_attention(Q, K, V)

max_diff = (ref - eff).abs().max().item()
print(f"Max absolute difference: {max_diff:.2e}")  # Should be < 1e-5
assert max_diff < 1e-4, f"Implementations diverge: {max_diff}"
print("Validation passed: implementations are numerically equivalent.")

From a security perspective, attention patterns can be used to probe model internals in model extraction attacks. An adversary with access to per-head attention weights can reverse-engineer aspects of the model's architecture and potentially extract training data. If serving in a multi-tenant environment, avoid returning attention weights in API responses.
Standard self-attention has O(n squared) time and memory in sequence length due to the n times n attention matrix, which is the bottleneck for long-context models.

FlashAttention achieves the same exact result as standard attention but uses IO-aware tiling to avoid materialising the full n times n matrix in HBM, giving 2 to 4 times speedup. Enable it via F.scaled_dot_product_attention in PyTorch 2.0 and later.

Grouped Query Attention reduces the KV cache by using G KV heads shared across Q heads (G less than H), directly reducing serving memory by H divided by G and enabling larger batch sizes. LLaMA-3 and Mistral use G=8 for H=32.
Sliding window attention limits each token to attending within a window of w neighbours, reducing complexity to O(n times w). Add global tokens to handle cross-window dependencies.
Linear attention and state-space models (Mamba) achieve O(n) complexity by approximating or replacing the softmax, at a quality cost that is task-dependent.

The practical priority order: enable FlashAttention first (free, exact), then adopt GQA for long-context inference (architecture decision at training time), then consider sparse attention for n above 32K.
Validate custom attention implementations against reference implementations before deployment; silent numerical errors in CUDA kernels are a real production risk.
